comment: Records all chat or inline Assistant interactions in order to see engagement and adoption.
business_context: Captures audit-style records for Databricks Assistant chat and inline interactions to measure engagement and adoption across accounts and workspaces. Useful for analyzing usage trends and user participation over time.
grain: One row per Assistant interaction event, uniquely identified by event_id (scoped within an account/workspace).
common_use_cases: Track Assistant adoption over time by workspace/account using event_time or event_date
Identify top users or teams engaging with Assistant via initiated_by
Segment usage by client/source using user_agent (e.g., UI vs other origins)
Join to workspaces_latest to enrich events with workspace metadata for reporting

columns.comment: ID of the account.
ID of the workspace.
A unique ID for this event.
columns.business_meaning: Unique identifier for a single Assistant interaction event record.

columns.comment: Time that the event happened. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
columns.business_meaning: Timestamp when the Assistant interaction occurred (with timezone offset).

columns.comment: Date that the event happened.
columns.business_meaning: Calendar date when the Assistant interaction occurred (derived from event_time).

columns.comment: Origination of request.
columns.business_meaning: Client/origin metadata for the request (e.g., application or surface where the Assistant was invoked).

columns.comment: Email of the user initiating the request.
columns.business_meaning: Email address of the user who initiated the Assistant interaction.

comment: The audit logs table records detailed information about actions and events occurring within a Databricks workspace or account. It is used for security auditing, compliance, and monitoring user and system activity. Each row represents a single event, such as a user login, data access, permission change, or system operation. See documentation of services and actions at https://docs.databricks.com/aws/admin/account-settings/audit-logs
business_context: System access audit logs capturing security- and compliance-relevant actions across Databricks account and workspaces. Used to monitor who did what, when, from where, and with what outcome for investigations and operational oversight.
grain: One row per audited event (single action/request) identified by event_id (and typically correlated with request_id and event_time) within an account/workspace.
common_use_cases: Security investigations and incident response (trace suspicious actions by user/session/IP)
Compliance reporting (access to data/permissions changes by date, service, action)
Operational monitoring (error rates and failed actions by service/action/status_code)
Correlation with other system tables (queries, jobs, clusters, billing usage) using workspace_id/request_id/event_id

columns.comment: The Databricks account identifier associated with the audit event.
The Databricks workspace in which the audit event took place. For account-level events, the workspace_id will be 0.
Audit log schema version
columns.business_meaning: Audit log schema version for the event record; helps interpret available fields and formats over time.

columns.comment: Timestamp of the event
The calendar date when the event or action occurred. Useful for filtering and aggregating events by day.
columns.business_meaning: Calendar date of the event, primarily for partitioning, filtering, and daily aggregations.

columns.comment: The IP address from which the request originated.
columns.business_meaning: Originating client IP address for the request that generated the audit event.

columns.comment: The user agent string or identifier describing the client, browser, or tool that initiated the request (e.g., web browser, API client, CLI)
columns.business_meaning: Client identifier string (browser/CLI/SDK/tool) used to make the request; useful for detecting automation vs interactive access.

columns.comment: Unique identifier for the session in which the request was made. Sessions group related actions by a user or service over a period of time
columns.business_meaning: Identifier that groups multiple audit events belonging to the same login/session context over a period of activity.

columns.comment: A map of key/value pairs that can be used to identify the person or service principal that initiated the request. Contains keys such as email and subject_name. An email of System-User is used for background system tasks.
columns.business_meaning: Identity attributes of the actor that initiated the event (for example email and subject_name), including system/background actors (e.g., System-User).

columns.comment: The name of the Databricks service that generated the audit event. See [service documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/admin/account-settings/audit-logs#audit-log-services) for details.
columns.business_meaning: Databricks product/service emitting the event (e.g., clusters, jobs, SQL, permissions), used to categorize activity.

columns.comment: The name of the action that has been performed as part of the audit event. Action names vary depending on the Databricks service (service_name). See [documentation of actions per service](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/admin/account-settings/audit-logs) for details.
columns.business_meaning: Action performed within the service (varies by service_name), used to identify the specific operation being audited.

columns.comment: A unique identifier of the request.
columns.business_meaning: Unique identifier for the underlying request, used to correlate related logs and downstream system records.

columns.comment: A map of key/value pairs with the request parameters. Request parameters vary by request type. See [documentation of request parameters per action](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/admin/account-settings/audit-logs) for details.
columns.business_meaning: Key/value parameters supplied with the request (shape varies by action); often contains resource identifiers and operation arguments.

columns.comment: Struct of response, includes status_code, error_messages, and where applicable a result string. status_code is an HTTP status code.
columns.business_meaning: Response details for the request, including HTTP status_code, any error_message, and an optional result summary.

columns.comment: Indicates whether the audit event is at the workspace or account level. Either `ACCOUNT_LEVEL` or `WORKSPACE_LEVEL`.
columns.business_meaning: Scope of the audit event (ACCOUNT_LEVEL vs WORKSPACE_LEVEL).

columns.comment: A unique identifier of the audit event.
columns.business_meaning: Unique identifier for the audit event record; serves as the natural primary key for the table.

columns.comment: A struct with the identities from the audit event. This includes `run_by` (the identity who initiated the event) and `run_as` (the identity being used for authorisation purposes). See [Auditing group dedicated compute activity documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/compute/group-access#auditing-group-dedicated-compute-activity) for more details.
columns.business_meaning: Additional identity context such as run_by (initiator), run_as (authorization identity), and acting_resource for delegated/impersonated or group compute scenarios.

comment: The clean_room_events table records actions taken by you or your collaborators on clean rooms (Public Preview) in your account. This table includes regional data from across your account.
business_context: Records audit-style events for Databricks Clean Rooms across the account, capturing who did what and when, plus event-specific metadata (creation, deletion, notebook runs, asset changes, reviews). Includes regional data aggregated across the account.
grain: One row per clean room event (uniquely identified by event_id) for a given account/metastore at a specific event_time.
common_use_cases: Audit collaborator actions on clean rooms (create/delete, asset changes, notebook runs)
Operational monitoring of clean room notebook executions and outcomes
Compliance reporting for clean room configuration and egress/compliance settings at creation time
Investigate asset review activity and approval signals over time

columns.comment: The ID of the Databricks account.
The ID of the Unity Catalog metastore.
The ID of the clean room event
columns.business_meaning: Unique identifier for the recorded clean room event.

columns.comment: Name of the clean room associated with the event
columns.business_meaning: Human-readable name of the clean room associated with the event.

columns.comment: The ID of central clean room
columns.business_meaning: Identifier of the central clean room entity that the event pertains to.

columns.comment: Global metastore ID of the collaborator who initiated the event
columns.business_meaning: Global metastore identifier of the collaborator who initiated the event.

columns.comment: The type of the event. See [Logged clean room events](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/clean-rooms.html#events).
columns.business_meaning: Categorical type of clean room event (for example: created, deleted, notebook run started/completed, assets updated, output schema deleted, asset review created).

columns.comment: The metadata of the event type CLEAN_ROOM_CREATED
columns.business_meaning: Event-specific payload populated for CLEAN_ROOM_CREATED, including collaborators and initial egress/compliance settings.

columns.comment: The metadata of the event type CLEAN_ROOM_DELETED
columns.business_meaning: Event-specific payload populated for CLEAN_ROOM_DELETED (includes the central clean room identifier being deleted).

columns.comment: The metadata of the event type RUN_NOTEBOOK_STARTED
columns.business_meaning: Event-specific payload populated for RUN_NOTEBOOK_STARTED, including notebook identity/checksum and the run identifier.

columns.comment: The metadata of the event type RUN_NOTEBOOK_COMPLETED
columns.business_meaning: Event-specific payload populated for RUN_NOTEBOOK_COMPLETED, including run state, duration, and any output schema/assets added.

columns.comment: The metadata of the event type CLEAN_ROOM_ASSETS_UPDATED
columns.business_meaning: Event-specific payload populated for CLEAN_ROOM_ASSETS_UPDATED, listing added/updated/removed assets and related notebook metadata.

columns.comment: Timestamp when the event took place
The metadata of the event type OUTPUT_SCHEMA_DELETED
columns.business_meaning: Event-specific payload populated for OUTPUT_SCHEMA_DELETED, describing the output schema removed and its expiration/action attributes.

columns.comment: The alias of the collaborator who initiated the event
columns.business_meaning: Display alias of the collaborator who initiated the event (useful for reporting and attribution).

columns.comment: The metadata of the event type ASSET_REVIEW_CREATED
columns.business_meaning: Event-specific payload populated for ASSET_REVIEW_CREATED, describing the asset under review, review state/reason, and any auto-approval rule reference.

comment: The column lineage system table includes a record for each write event (Spark operation) on a Unity Catalog table or path. This includes but is not limited to job, notebook, and dashboard runs. The column lineage table does not include events that do not have a source (e.g. inserting explicit values). Please refer to the [documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/data-lineage.html#limitations) for limitations.
business_context: System table capturing column-level lineage for each Spark write event affecting a Unity Catalog table or path, including runs from notebooks, jobs, pipelines, and dashboards. It links source columns/objects to target columns/objects for governance, impact analysis, and auditing.
grain: One row per lineage mapping from a single source column (or source object) to a single target column for a specific write event; natural keys include record_id (row PK) and event_id (groups rows for the same event), with optional entity_type/entity_id/entity_run_id context.
common_use_cases: Trace which upstream columns and sources feed a given target column (impact analysis)
Attribute data transformations to the originating job/notebook/query run for auditing
Build end-to-end lineage graphs by joining to table_lineage and query history (statement_id)
Monitor changes in lineage over time by event_date/event_time and created_by

columns.comment: The id of the Databricks account.
The id of the Unity Catalog metastore.
The id of the workspace
The type of entity the lineage transaction was captured from. The supported value is NOTEBOOK, JOB, PIPELINE, DASHBOARD_V3 (AI/BI Dashboard), DBSQL_DASHBOARD (Legacy dashboard), DBSQL_QUERY, OR NULL.
columns.business_meaning: Type of originating context where the lineage event was captured (for example NOTEBOOK, JOB, PIPELINE, DASHBOARD, DBSQL_QUERY), or NULL when unavailable.

columns.comment: The id of the entity the lineage transaction was captured from. If entity_type is NULL, entity_id is NULL.
columns.business_meaning: Identifier of the originating entity (such as notebook, job, pipeline, dashboard, or SQL query) that produced the write event; NULL if entity_type is NULL.

columns.comment:
id to describe the unique run of the entity, or NULL. This differs for each entity type:

Notebook: command_run_id

Job: job_run_id

Databricks SQL query: statement_id

Dashboard: statement_id

Legacy dashboard: statement_id

Pipeline: pipeline_update_id

If entity_type is NULL, entity_run_id is NULL. Records with statement_id and job_run_id can be joined with the query history and jobs system tables respectively.
columns.business_meaning: Identifier for the specific run/execution of the originating entity (for example job_run_id, statement_id, pipeline_update_id, command_run_id), or NULL when not applicable/unknown.

columns.comment: Three-level name to identify the source table.
columns.business_meaning: Three-level Unity Catalog name (catalog.schema.table) of the source table when the source is a UC table.

columns.comment: The catalog of the source table.
columns.business_meaning: Catalog containing the source table (when the source is a UC table).

columns.comment: The schema of the source table.
columns.business_meaning: Schema containing the source table (when the source is a UC table).

columns.comment: The name of the source table.
columns.business_meaning: Unqualified name of the source table (when the source is a UC table).

columns.comment: Location in cloud storage of the source table, or the path if it’s reading from cloud storage directly.
columns.business_meaning: Cloud storage location of the source table, or the input path when reading directly from storage rather than a table.

columns.comment: The type of the source. The value is TABLE, PATH, VIEW, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, METRIC_VIEW, or STREAMING_TABLE.
columns.business_meaning: Classification of the source object (TABLE, PATH, VIEW, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, METRIC_VIEW, or STREAMING_TABLE).

columns.comment: The name of the source column.
columns.business_meaning: Name of the source column contributing to the target column for this lineage record.

columns.comment: Three-level name to identify the target table.
columns.business_meaning: Three-level Unity Catalog name (catalog.schema.table) of the target table written by the event when the target is a UC table.

columns.comment: The catalog of the target table.
columns.business_meaning: Catalog containing the target table (when the target is a UC table).

columns.comment: The schema of the target table.
columns.business_meaning: Schema containing the target table (when the target is a UC table).

columns.comment: The name of the target table.
columns.business_meaning: Unqualified name of the target table (when the target is a UC table).

columns.comment: Location in cloud storage of the target table.
columns.business_meaning: Cloud storage location of the target table or written path.

columns.comment: The type of the target. The value is TABLE, PATH, VIEW, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, METRIC_VIEW, or STREAMING_TABLE.
columns.business_meaning: Classification of the target object (TABLE, PATH, VIEW, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, METRIC_VIEW, or STREAMING_TABLE).

columns.comment: The name of the target column.
columns.business_meaning: Name of the target column produced/updated by the write event for this lineage record.

columns.comment: The user who generated this lineage. This can be a Databricks username, a Databricks service principal ID, “System-User”, or NULL if the user information cannot be captured.
columns.business_meaning: User or service principal responsible for generating the lineage event; may be System-User or NULL if not captured.

columns.comment: The timestamp when the lineage was generated. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
columns.business_meaning: Timestamp when the lineage record was generated for the write event (timezone included in the stored value).

columns.comment: The date when the lineage was generated. This is a partitioned column.
columns.business_meaning: Event date for the lineage generation time; used for partitioning.

columns.comment: Primary key of each row, it is auto-generated and cannot be joined with any tables
columns.business_meaning: Auto-generated primary key for the lineage row; intended as a unique row identifier and not joinable to other tables.

columns.comment: One query or one spark job run could append multiple lineage rows, this event_id is a unique id to group the rows that belong to the same event. This is generated in the pipeline and cannot be joined with any tables.
columns.business_meaning: Identifier to group multiple lineage rows produced by the same write event (single query or Spark job run); generated internally and not joinable to other tables.

columns.comment: A foreign key to join with query history system table. It is set when a query is from a warehouse or serverless warehouse.
columns.business_meaning: Foreign key to Databricks SQL query history for warehouse/serverless executions; populated when the event originates from a SQL statement context.

columns.comment: It is a list of ids of the query context which is joinable with other system tables.
columns.business_meaning: Structured context identifiers for the originating workload (for example job_id/job_run_id, notebook_id, sql_query_id, dashboard ids, and DLT pipeline/update ids) to support joins to other system tables.

comment: The inbound network access control table records policy evaluation events for incoming network requests. Each row represents a single access control policy evaluation.
business_context: This table captures inbound network access-control policy evaluation events for incoming requests, useful for monitoring and auditing network access decisions. Each record reflects the outcome and context of evaluating access policies for a single inbound request.
grain: One row per inbound network request access-control policy evaluation event.
common_use_cases: Audit inbound network requests and the associated access-control decisions
Investigate denied inbound requests to identify blocked sources or misconfigurations
Monitor trends in inbound access decisions over time (allow vs deny)

comment: The network access events table records events where internet access is denied. Each row represents an individual event. For example, if a user attempts to access “google.com” from a notebook and fails, the failure would be logged as an event.
business_context: Records outbound network access attempts that were denied from Databricks compute, capturing what destination was blocked and when it occurred. Used to audit and troubleshoot egress restrictions and blocked internet/storage access.
grain: One row per denied outbound network access event (uniquely identified by event_id) within a Databricks account and workspace.
common_use_cases: Audit denied egress events by workspace and time window for security/compliance reporting
Troubleshoot why a domain/IP/storage endpoint is unreachable from notebooks/jobs
Identify most frequently blocked destinations and destination types to refine network policies
Correlate denial patterns with specific access types and destination details (DNS vs storage)

columns.comment: The ID of the Databricks account
The ID of the workspace where the event occurred
The type of destination. Possible values are DNS, IP, and STORAGE
columns.business_meaning: Category of blocked destination targeted by the outbound request (e.g., DNS name lookup, direct IP, or storage endpoint).

columns.comment: Details of the blocked destination. Depending on the destination type, the value could be a domain name, IP address, or storage location.
columns.business_meaning: The blocked destination value, interpreted based on destination_type (domain name, IP address, or storage location/endpoint).

columns.comment: Details about the DNS destination. Only populates for DNS destinations, otherwise the field is NULL.
columns.business_meaning: Populated only when destination_type is DNS; contains DNS-specific details such as queried domain and response code.

columns.comment: Details about the storage destination. Only populates for storage destinations, otherwise the field is NULL.
columns.business_meaning: Populated only when destination_type is STORAGE; contains storage endpoint details (hostname/path) and reason the request was rejected.

columns.comment: Timestamp when the event took place
Type of access event that occurred.
columns.business_meaning: Label for the kind of outbound access attempt that was denied (e.g., the access mechanism or request category used by the platform).

columns.comment: The ID of the event
The source type of the network event, mainly for customer debugging
columns.business_meaning: Origin classification of the network event used primarily for debugging where the blocked request came from within the platform.

comment: The table lineage system table includes a record for each read or write event (Spark operation) on a UC table or path. This includes but is not limited to job, notebook, and dashboard runs. This table can be joined with other system tables such as Query History and Jobs to get additional information about the read/write operation. Please refer to the [documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/data-lineage.html#limitations) for limitations.

relationships.core_columns: system.access.table_lineage.job_id = system.billing.usage.job_id AND system.access.table_lineage.job_run_id = system.billing.usage.job_run_id AND system.access.table_lineage.workspace_id = system.billing.usage.workspace_id

business_context: Records read/write lineage events captured from Spark operations against Unity Catalog tables or direct paths, across notebooks, jobs, pipelines, and SQL assets. Used to trace data movement and dependencies and to enrich lineage with execution metadata via joins to query history, jobs, pipelines, and audit/billing tables.
grain: One row per lineage edge captured for a single read or write event, linking a source (table/view/path) to a target (table/view/path) within a specific entity run; primary key is record_id, with event_id grouping multiple rows belonging to the same execution event.
common_use_cases: Build end-to-end lineage graphs from upstream sources to downstream targets for governance and impact analysis
Investigate who/what wrote to a table or path and from which inputs, at a specific time or run
Join to query history/jobs/pipelines to attribute lineage to executions and operational metadata (status, duration, cluster/warehouse)
Allocate costs/usage to downstream assets by connecting billing/usage to the producing queries/jobs via statement_id or run identifiers

columns.comment: The id of the Databricks account.
The id of the Unity Catalog metastore.
The id of the workspace
The type of entity the lineage transaction was captured from. The value is NOTEBOOK, JOB, PIPELINE, DASHBOARD_V3 (Dashboard), DBSQL_DASHBOARD (Legacy dashboard), DBSQL_QUERY, OR NULL.
columns.business_meaning: Originating context that produced the Spark operation (for example NOTEBOOK, JOB, PIPELINE, or SQL/dashboard assets).

columns.comment: The ID of the entity the lineage transaction was captured from. If entity_type is NULL, entity_id is NULL.
columns.business_meaning: Identifier of the originating entity (e.g., notebook/job/pipeline/dashboard/query) when available; NULL when entity_type is NULL.

columns.comment:
id to describe the unique run of the entity, or NULL. This differs for each entity type:

Notebook: command_run_id

Job: job_run_id

Databricks SQL query: statement_id

Dashboard: statement_id

Legacy dashboard: statement_id

Pipeline: pipeline_update_id

If entity_type is NULL, entity_run_id is NULL. Records with statement_id and job_run_id can be joined with the query history and jobs system tables respectively.
columns.business_meaning: Identifier for the specific run/execution of the originating entity (e.g., job_run_id, statement_id, pipeline_update_id), when available.

columns.comment: Three-part name to identify the source table.
columns.business_meaning: Three-part Unity Catalog name of the upstream source table when the source is a UC table/view-like object.

columns.comment: The catalog of the source table.
The schema of the source table.
The name of the source table.
Location in cloud storage of the source table, or the path if it’s reading from cloud storage directly.
columns.business_meaning: Cloud storage location read from, either the backing location of a table or a direct path read.

columns.comment: The type of the source. The value is TABLE, PATH, VIEW, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, METRIC_VIEW, or STREAMING_TABLE.
columns.business_meaning: Classification of the upstream source being read (TABLE, PATH, VIEW, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, METRIC_VIEW, STREAMING_TABLE).

columns.comment: Three-part name to identify the target table.
columns.business_meaning: Three-part Unity Catalog name of the downstream target table when the target is a UC table/view-like object.

columns.comment: The catalog of the target table.
The schema of the target table.
The name of the target table.
Location in cloud storage of the target table.
columns.business_meaning: Cloud storage location written to (typically the backing location of the target table or a direct path write).

columns.comment: The type of the target. The value is TABLE, PATH, VIEW, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, METRIC_VIEW, or STREAMING_TABLE.
columns.business_meaning: Classification of the downstream target being written (TABLE, PATH, VIEW, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, METRIC_VIEW, STREAMING_TABLE).

columns.comment: The user who generated this lineage. This can be a Databricks username, a Databricks service principal ID, “System-User”, or NULL if the user information cannot be captured.
columns.business_meaning: Actor that triggered the operation (user, service principal, System-User), when identity can be captured.

columns.comment: The timestamp when the lineage was generated. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
columns.business_meaning: Timestamp when the lineage record was generated for the operation.

columns.comment: The date when the lineage was generated. This is a partitioned column.
columns.business_meaning: Calendar date of lineage generation, used for partitioning and date-based filtering.

columns.comment: Primary key of each row, it is auto-generated and cannot be joined with any tables
columns.business_meaning: System-generated primary key for the lineage row.

columns.comment: One query or one spark job run could append multiple lineage rows, this event_id is a unique id to group the rows that belong to the same event. This is generated in the pipeline and cannot be joined with any tables.
columns.business_meaning: System-generated identifier to group multiple lineage rows that belong to the same underlying execution event.

columns.comment: A foreign key to join with query history system table. It is set when a query is from a warehouse or serverless warehouse.
columns.business_meaning: Databricks SQL statement execution identifier used to join to query history when the operation ran on a (serverless) SQL warehouse.

columns.comment: It is a list of ids of the query context which is joinable with other system tables.
columns.business_meaning: Nested identifiers describing the execution context (e.g., job/job_run, notebook, SQL query, dashboards, DLT pipeline/update) to support joins and attribution.

comment: The workspaces system table is a slow-changing dimension table of metadata for all the workspaces in your account.
business_context: Slow-changing dimension containing the latest known metadata for every Databricks workspace in an account, used to enrich other system tables with workspace attributes and status.
grain: One row per workspace_id (current/latest record for that workspace) within an account_id.
common_use_cases: Join to billing usage to attribute costs/consumption to workspace name and URL
Filter or report on active vs non-active workspaces using lifecycle status
Enrich audit logs with workspace metadata for governance and investigations
Create an inventory of workspaces in the account with creation timestamps

columns.comment: The ID of the parent account of this workspace.
The ID of the workspace.
The human-readable name of the workspace.
URL of the databricks workspace.
Timestamp of when the workspace was created.
Lifecycle status of the workspace: NOT_PROVISIONED | PROVISIONING | RUNNING | FAILED | BANNED
columns.business_meaning: Current lifecycle state of the workspace (e.g., provisioning, running, failed, banned).

business_context: Contains time-bounded list prices for Databricks SKUs at the account level, including cloud, currency, and usage unit. Used to price metered usage by applying the effective rate for the usage timestamp.
grain: One row per account_id, sku_name, cloud, currency_code, usage_unit, and price effective interval (price_start_time to price_end_time).
common_use_cases: Apply the correct unit price to usage records based on usage time and SKU
Audit historical price changes and effective-date pricing timelines
Validate billing totals by recomputing charges from usage and price tables
Support financial reporting by normalizing costs to the billed currency and unit

columns.comment: ID of the account this report was generated for
The time this price became effective in UTC
columns.business_meaning: UTC timestamp when this price becomes effective for the given SKU and dimensions.

columns.comment: The time this price stopped being effective in UTC
columns.business_meaning: UTC timestamp when this price is no longer effective (often null/open-ended for the current price).

columns.comment: Name of the SKU
Name of the Cloud this price is applicable to. Possible values are AWS, AZURE, and GCP.
columns.business_meaning: Cloud provider the price applies to (e.g., AWS, AZURE, GCP).

columns.comment: The currency this price is expressed in
The unit of measurement that is monetized.
columns.business_meaning: Unit of measure that the SKU is priced in (the unit the default price applies to).
Structured pricing attributes for the SKU and interval; includes the default unit price rate.

comment: The [pricing table](https://docs.databricks.com/admin/system-tables/pricing.html) gives you access to a historical log of SKU pricing. A record gets added each time there is a change to a SKU price.
business_context: Historical reference table of Databricks SKU list prices by cloud and currency; a new row is emitted whenever a SKU price changes. It is used to price system.billing.usage records for accurate cost reporting over time.
grain: One row per (account_id, sku_name, cloud, currency_code, usage_unit) price interval, effective from price_start_time until price_end_time.
common_use_cases: Join to system.billing.usage to compute costs using the price effective at the usage time
Analyze historical SKU price changes over time by cloud/currency
Build forecasting/what-if models using default vs promotional vs effective list pricing
Validate invoices or internal chargeback rates against published list pricing

columns.comment: ID of the account this report was generated for
The time this price became effective in UTC
columns.business_meaning: UTC timestamp when this price becomes effective for the SKU in the given cloud/currency/unit.

columns.comment: The time this price stopped being effective in UTC
columns.business_meaning: UTC timestamp when this price stops being effective; typically null or a later time until superseded by a newer price.

columns.comment: Name of the SKU
Name of the Cloud this price is applicable to. Possible values are AWS, AZURE, and GCP.
columns.business_meaning: Cloud provider the price applies to (AWS, AZURE, or GCP).

columns.comment: The currency this price is expressed in
The unit of measurement that is monetized.
columns.business_meaning: Billable unit of measure that the price is quoted in (the unit used to monetize usage).

columns.comment: A structured data field that includes pricing info at the published list price rate. The key default will always return a single price that can be used for simple long-term estimates. The key promotional represents a temporary promotional price that all customers get which could be used for cost estimation during the temporary period. The key effective_list resolves list and promotional price, and contains the effective list price used for calculating the cost.
columns.business_meaning: Nested price details at list rate: default is the standard list price for long-term estimates; promotional.default is a temporary promotional list price; effective_list.default is the resolved list price actually used for cost calculation during the interval.

comment: The [usage table](https://docs.databricks.com/admin/system-tables/billing.html) gives you access to account-wide billable usage data. The data is centralized and routed to all the regions.

business_context: Centralized, account-wide billable usage records across all Databricks workspaces and regions, used to attribute consumption to products, compute resources, and identities for cost and chargeback reporting. Includes correction mechanics (retractions/restatements) and rich metadata to join to pricing and platform objects.
grain: One billable usage line item for an account/workspace over a specific usage time window (usage_start_time to usage_end_time), uniquely identified by record_id; corrections may appear as additional records via record_type.
common_use_cases: Compute daily/monthly spend and DBU consumption by workspace, SKU, and product (with pricing joins to list_prices/account_prices).
Allocate/charge back costs to clusters, warehouses, jobs, pipelines, endpoints, or Unity Catalog objects using usage_metadata.
Detect cost anomalies and explain drivers by usage_type, feature flags (serverless/photon), and identity attribution.
Build billing correction-aware reports by netting ORIGINAL vs RETRACTION/RESTATEMENT records.

columns.comment: ID of the account this report was generated for
ID of the Workspace this usage was associated with
Unique ID for this usage record
columns.business_meaning: Unique identifier for the usage line item (used as the primary key for the record).

columns.comment: Name of the SKU
columns.business_meaning: Billable SKU the usage is charged under (join key to price lists).

columns.comment: Cloud this usage is relevant for. Possible values are AWS, AZURE, and GCP.
columns.business_meaning: Cloud provider where the usage occurred (AWS, AZURE, or GCP).

columns.comment: The start time relevant to this usage record. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC timezone.
columns.business_meaning: Start timestamp of the time window covered by this usage record (timezone encoded in the value).

columns.comment: The end time relevant to this usage record. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC timezone.
columns.business_meaning: End timestamp of the time window covered by this usage record (timezone encoded in the value).

columns.comment: Date of the usage record, this field can be used for faster aggregation by date
columns.business_meaning: Calendar date associated with the usage record, intended for date-bucket aggregation.

columns.comment: Tags applied by the users to this usage. Includes compute resource tags and jobs tags.
columns.business_meaning: User-applied tags attached to the usage (for example, compute resource tags and job tags) to support cost allocation.

columns.comment: Unit this usage is measured in. Possible values include DBUs.
columns.business_meaning: Measurement unit for usage_quantity (for example, DBUs).

columns.comment: Number of units consumed for this record.
columns.business_meaning: Amount of consumption in usage_unit for this record.

columns.comment: System-provided metadata about the usage, including IDs for compute resources and jobs (if applicable). See [Analyze usage metadata](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/billing.html#usage-metadata).
columns.business_meaning: System-provided attribution metadata for the usage (for example, cluster/job/warehouse identifiers and other workload-specific details) used for joining and chargeback.

columns.comment: System-provided metadata about the identities involved in the usage. See [Analyze identity metadata](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/billing.html#identity-metadata).
columns.business_meaning: System-provided identity attribution involved in generating the usage (run_as/created_by/owned_by).

columns.comment: Whether the record is original, a retraction, or a restatement. The value is ORIGINAL unless the record is related to a correction. See [Analyze correction records](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/billing.html#record-type).
columns.business_meaning: Correction classification for the usage record (ORIGINAL vs correction-related types such as retraction or restatement).

columns.comment: Date the record was ingested into the usage table.
columns.business_meaning: Date the usage record was ingested into the system table (useful for incremental processing and latency monitoring).

columns.comment: The product that originated the usage. Some products can be billed as different SKUs. For possible values, see [View information about the product associated with the usage](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/billing.html#features).
columns.business_meaning: Databricks product that originated the usage (may map to different billable SKUs).

columns.comment: Details about the specific product features used.
columns.business_meaning: Structured feature attributes describing how the product/workload was configured (for example, tiers, serverless/photon flags, serving type, connectivity, AI/runtime characteristics).

columns.comment: The type of usage attributed to the product or workload for billing purposes. Possible values are COMPUTE_TIME, STORAGE_SPACE, NETWORK_BYTES, API_CALLS, TOKEN, or GPU_TIME.
columns.business_meaning: Billing attribution category for the consumption (for example, COMPUTE_TIME, STORAGE_SPACE, NETWORK_BYTES, API_CALLS, TOKEN, GPU_TIME).

comment: The cluster table is a slow-changing dimension table that contains the full history of compute configurations over time for all-purpose and jobs compute.
business_context: Slow-changing dimension capturing the full historical configuration of Databricks compute (all-purpose and jobs) across workspaces and accounts. Use it to understand how cluster settings changed over time and to enrich operational, audit, and billing datasets with cluster metadata.
grain: One row per cluster configuration version (SCD record) for a given cluster_id within a workspace/account, effective at change_time (with deletion indicated by delete_time when applicable).
common_use_cases: Enrich billing usage with cluster configuration (node types, DBR version, autoscaling settings) at the time of usage
Track cluster ownership, naming, and policy/security mode changes over time for governance and audit investigations
Analyze compute fleet standardization (node types, instance pools, tags) and drift from policies
Join to node_timeline to relate configuration versions to underlying node lifecycle events

columns.comment: ID of the account where this cluster was created.
ID of the workspace where this cluster was created.

User defined name for the cluster.

columns.comment: Username of the cluster owner. Defaults to the cluster creator, but can be changed through the [Clusters API](https://docs.databricks.com/api/workspace/clusters/changeowner).
columns.business_meaning: Username of the current cluster owner for this configuration version (may differ from creator if ownership was changed).

columns.comment: Timestamp of the change to this compute definition.
columns.business_meaning: Timestamp associated with creation of this configuration record/definition change as recorded by the system.

columns.comment: Timestamp of when the cluster was deleted. The value is null if the cluster is not deleted.
columns.business_meaning: When the cluster was deleted; null indicates the cluster was not deleted as of this record.

columns.comment: Driver node type name. This matches the instance type name from the cloud provider.

columns.business_meaning: Cloud instance type used for worker nodes in this cluster configuration.

columns.business_meaning: Lower bound of workers when autoscaling is enabled.

columns.comment: The set maximum number of workers. This field is valid only for autoscaling clusters.
columns.business_meaning: Upper bound of workers when autoscaling is enabled.

columns.comment: The configured autotermination duration.
columns.business_meaning: Idle time (in minutes) after which the cluster is configured to automatically terminate.

columns.comment: Autoscaling disk enablement status.
columns.business_meaning: Whether elastic disk (automatic disk expansion) is enabled for the cluster.

columns.comment: User-defined tags for the cluster (does not include default tags).
columns.business_meaning: User-defined tag key/value pairs applied to the cluster (excluding default/system tags).

columns.comment: Where the cluster was created. For all-purpose compute, the source could be `UI` or `API`. Other compute types are logged as `JOB`, `PIPELINE`, or `PIPELINE_MAINTENANCE`.
columns.business_meaning: Origin/type of cluster creation (e.g., UI/API for all-purpose; JOB/PIPELINE variants for automated compute).

columns.comment: Set of paths for init scripts.
columns.business_meaning: Configured init script paths executed during cluster startup.

columns.comment: AWS specific settings.
columns.business_meaning: AWS-specific cluster configuration settings (e.g., availability, zone, instance profile, and EBS/spot parameters).

columns.comment: Azure specific settings.
columns.business_meaning: Azure-specific cluster configuration settings (e.g., availability and spot/on-demand parameters).

columns.comment: GCP specific settings.
columns.business_meaning: GCP-specific cluster configuration settings (e.g., preemptible executors, service account, disks, and zone/availability).

columns.comment: Instance pool ID if the driver is configured on top of an instance pool.
columns.business_meaning: Instance pool identifier used to provision the driver, when configured.

columns.comment: Instance Pool ID if the worker is configured on top of an instance pool.
columns.business_meaning: Instance pool identifier used to provision workers, when configured.

columns.comment: The Databricks Runtime of the cluster.
columns.business_meaning: Databricks Runtime version configured for the cluster (runtime/scala/spark compatibility identifier).

columns.comment: Timestamp of change to the compute definition.
columns.business_meaning: Timestamp when this cluster configuration version became effective (i.e., when the compute definition changed).

columns.comment: Change date. Used for retention.
columns.business_meaning: Calendar date corresponding to change_time, primarily used for retention/partitioning.

columns.comment: Data security mode decides what data governance model to use when accessing data from a cluster.
columns.business_meaning: Security/governance mode determining the cluster’s data access model (e.g., isolation and credential passthrough behaviors).

columns.comment: The ID of the cluster policy used to create the cluster if applicable.
columns.business_meaning: Cluster policy identifier that governed creation/configuration of the cluster, if one was applied.

comment: The node timeline table captures node-level resource utilization data at minute granularity. Each record contains data for a given minute of time per instance.
business_context: Minute-by-minute node-level resource utilization telemetry for Databricks compute instances. It supports operational monitoring, cost/usage attribution, and troubleshooting performance issues at the instance (driver/worker) level.
grain: One row per account_id + workspace_id + cluster_id + instance_id + minute time window (start_time to end_time).
common_use_cases: Identify CPU, memory, disk, or network bottlenecks on specific clusters and nodes over time
Attribute resource saturation patterns to workloads and correlate with billing usage by cluster/workspace
Detect unhealthy nodes (e.g., high I/O wait, heavy swap, low free disk) and trigger remediation
Compare driver vs worker utilization to diagnose skew or driver pressure

columns.comment: ID of the account where this compute resource is running.
ID of the workspace where this compute resource is running.
ID of the compute resource.
ID for the specific instance.
columns.business_meaning: Identifier of the individual VM/node within the cluster for which metrics are reported.

columns.comment: Start time for the record in UTC.
End time for the record in UTC.
Whether the instance is a driver or worker node.
columns.business_meaning: Indicates whether the node is the cluster driver (true) or a worker (false).

columns.comment: Percentage of time the CPU spent in userland.
columns.business_meaning: CPU utilization percentage spent executing user-space (non-kernel) code during the time window.

columns.comment: Percentage of time the CPU spent in the kernel.
columns.business_meaning: CPU utilization percentage spent in kernel/system operations during the time window.

columns.comment: Percentage of time the CPU spent waiting for I/O.
columns.business_meaning: CPU time percentage spent waiting on I/O (e.g., disk or network) during the time window.

columns.comment: Percentage of the compute’s memory that was used during the time period (including memory used by background processes running on the compute).
columns.business_meaning: Percentage of total node memory used during the time window, including background/system processes.

columns.comment: Percentage of memory usage attributed to memory swap.
columns.business_meaning: Percentage of memory usage attributable to swapping, indicating memory pressure.

columns.comment: The number of bytes sent out in network traffic.
columns.business_meaning: Total bytes transmitted from the node over the network during the time window.

columns.comment: The number of received bytes from network traffic.
columns.business_meaning: Total bytes received by the node over the network during the time window.

columns.comment: The disk utilization grouped by mount point. This is ephemeral storage provisioned only while the compute is running.
columns.business_meaning: Map of mount point to free bytes for the node’s ephemeral disks during the time window.

columns.comment: The name of the node type. This will match the instance type name from the cloud provider.
columns.business_meaning: Cloud instance type / node SKU used for the VM (matches the provider’s instance type name).

columns.comment: Private IP.
columns.business_meaning: Node private IP address within the VPC/VNet at the time of measurement.

comment: The node type table captures the currently available node types with their basic hardware information
business_context: Lists the node (instance) types currently available in a Databricks account along with basic hardware specifications. Useful for understanding and selecting compute options for clusters and SQL warehouses.
grain: One row per (account_id, node_type) representing a distinct available node type and its hardware profile.
common_use_cases: Populate UI or catalog of available node types for provisioning compute
Validate cluster/warehouse configurations against available node types
Compare hardware characteristics (CPU, memory, GPU) across node types
Filter node types for workload requirements (e.g., GPU-enabled, minimum memory)

columns.comment: ID of the account where this node type is available.
Unique identifier for node type.
columns.business_meaning: Databricks node type identifier used to select a specific instance shape when provisioning compute.

columns.comment: Number of vCPUs for the instance.
columns.business_meaning: Number of virtual CPU cores provided by the node type.

columns.comment: Total memory for the instance.
columns.business_meaning: Total RAM provided by the node type, measured in megabytes.

columns.comment: Number of GPUs for the instance.
columns.business_meaning: Number of GPUs attached to the node type (0 for non-GPU shapes).

comment: The Warehouse events table table captures events related to SQL warehouses. For example, starting, stopping, running, scaling up and down
business_context: Captures lifecycle and scaling events emitted by Databricks SQL Warehouses, enabling operational monitoring and correlation with audit and query activity. Used to understand when a warehouse started/stopped or changed size and what capacity was running at those times.
grain: One row per SQL warehouse event (identified by warehouse_id + event_time + event_type) within an account and workspace.
common_use_cases: Build timelines of warehouse start/stop and scaling activity for operational monitoring
Correlate warehouse events with query history to explain performance or availability changes
Analyze autoscaling behavior (scale up/down frequency) and active cluster counts over time
Join to warehouse metadata to report events by warehouse name, size, or configuration

columns.comment: The ID of the Databricks account.
The ID of the workspace where the warehouse is deployed.
The ID of SQL warehouse the event is related to.
The type of warehouse event. Possible values are SCALED_UP, SCALED_DOWN, STOPPING, RUNNING, STARTING, and STOPPED.
columns.business_meaning: Categorical warehouse event indicating a lifecycle transition or scaling action (for example STARTING, RUNNING, STOPPING, STOPPED, SCALED_UP, SCALED_DOWN).

columns.comment: The number of clusters that are actively running.
columns.business_meaning: Number of warehouse clusters actively running at the time of the event, reflecting current capacity/concurrency for the warehouse.

columns.comment: Timestamp of when the event took place in UTC.
columns.business_meaning: UTC timestamp when the warehouse event occurred.

comment: The Warehouses table contains the full history of configurations over time for any SQL warehouse.
business_context: Stores the full configuration history for Databricks SQL warehouses, capturing each change to a warehouse’s definition over time. Useful for governance, auditing, and correlating warehouse settings with operational events and query workloads.
grain: One row per warehouse configuration version at a point in time (identified by warehouse_id and change_time) within a workspace/account.
common_use_cases: Audit configuration changes (size, auto-stop, min/max clusters, type/channel) over time for a warehouse
Reconstruct point-in-time warehouse settings when investigating performance or cost anomalies
Join to warehouse_events and query history to correlate settings with incidents and workload behavior
Inventory and governance reporting across workspaces/accounts, including deleted warehouses

columns.comment: The ID of the SQL warehouse.
The ID of the workspace where the warehouse is deployed.
The ID of the Databricks account.
The name of the SQL warehouse.
The type of SQL warehouse. Possible values are CLASSIC, PRO, and SERVERLESS.
columns.business_meaning: SKU/class of the SQL warehouse (e.g., Classic, Pro, Serverless) that influences capabilities and billing/operation model.

columns.comment: The channel of the SQL warehouse. Possible values are CURRENT and PREVIEW.
columns.business_meaning: Release channel for the warehouse runtime/feature set (e.g., Current vs Preview).

columns.comment: The cluster size of the SQL warehouse. Possible values are 2X_SMALL, X_SMALL, SMALL, MEDIUM, LARGE, X_LARGE, 2X_LARGE, 3X_LARGE, 4X_LARGE, and 5X_LARGE.
columns.business_meaning: Configured compute size tier for the warehouse, used as the baseline capacity for serving SQL workloads.

columns.comment: The minimum number of clusters permitted.
columns.business_meaning: Lower bound on the number of clusters the warehouse can run (minimum scaling floor).

columns.comment: The maximum number of clusters permitted.
columns.business_meaning: Upper bound on the number of clusters the warehouse can scale out to (maximum scaling ceiling).

columns.comment: The number of minutes before the SQL warehouse auto-stops due to inactivity.
columns.business_meaning: Idle time threshold (in minutes) after which the warehouse is automatically stopped to reduce cost.

columns.comment: Tags for the SQL warehouse.
columns.business_meaning: Key/value metadata attached to the warehouse for organization, chargeback, and policy enforcement.

columns.comment: Timestamp of change to the SQL warehouse definition.
columns.business_meaning: Timestamp when this configuration version became effective (i.e., when the warehouse definition was changed).

columns.comment: Timestamp of when the SQL warehouse was deleted. The value is null if the SQL warehouse is not deleted.
columns.business_meaning: Timestamp when the warehouse was deleted; null indicates the warehouse had not been deleted as of this configuration record.

columns.comment: The name of the Databricks principal or user that created this warehouse.
columns.business_meaning: Principal (user/service principal) recorded as the creator of the warehouse.

comment: The `job_run_timeline` table is a fact table that contains slices of ongoing job runs, allowing one to track the run time and metadata related to each job run. Each slice is represented by `period_start_time` and `period_end_time` timestamps. For documentation, example queries, and monitoring usage, see [Lakeflow System Tables](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#job-run-timeline-table-schema).

extended_conditions.condition: system.billing.usage.job_id = system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline.job_id AND system.billing.usage.job_run_id = system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline.run_id AND system.billing.usage.workspace_id = system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline.workspace_id

system.billing.usage.job_id = system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline.job_id AND system.billing.usage.run_id = system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline.run_id AND system.billing.usage.workspace_id = system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline.workspace_id

business_context: System fact table that breaks each Databricks job run into time slices, enabling timeline-based monitoring and correlation to compute usage and billing. It supports tracking run progress, outcomes, and run metadata over the course of execution.
grain: One row per (account_id, workspace_id, job_id, run_id, period_start_time, period_end_time) time slice for a job run.
common_use_cases: Compute run-time and wall-clock duration trends for jobs and runs over time
Join to billing usage and compute/cluster metadata to attribute cost and resource usage to job runs
Monitor failures and termination reasons (result_state, termination_code/type) for alerting and reliability reporting
Analyze queue/setup/execution/cleanup phase durations to identify orchestration and performance bottlenecks

columns.comment: The ID of the account this job belongs to.
The ID of the workspace this job belongs to.
The ID of the job. This key is only unique within a single workspace.
columns.business_meaning: Workspace-scoped identifier of the Job definition that produced the run.

columns.comment: The ID of the job run.
columns.business_meaning: Identifier of a specific execution instance of the job; multiple timeline slices can exist per run.

columns.comment: The start time for the run or for the time period. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
columns.business_meaning: Inclusive start timestamp of the time slice for the job run (UTC offset included).

columns.comment: The end time for the run or for the time period. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
columns.business_meaning: Exclusive (or slice end) timestamp of the time slice for the job run (UTC offset included).

columns.comment: The type of trigger that can fire a run. For possible values, see [Trigger type values](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#trigger)
columns.business_meaning: How the run was initiated (for example, manual, scheduled, file-arrival, continuous, or API-triggered depending on system values).

columns.comment: The outcome of the job run. For possible values, see [Result state values](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#result).
columns.business_meaning: Final outcome state of the run when available (for example, success, failed, canceled, timed out).

columns.comment: The type of job run. For possible values, see [Run type values](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#run-type).
columns.business_meaning: Classification of the run execution type (for example, workflow run vs other system-defined run types).

columns.comment: The user-supplied run name associated with this job run.
columns.business_meaning: Human-readable, user-supplied label for the run instance.

columns.comment: Array containing the compute IDs for the parent job run. Use for identifying cluster used by `WORKFLOW_RUN` run types. For other compute information, refer to the `job_task_run_timeline` table. **Not populated for rows emitted before late August 2024**
columns.business_meaning: List of compute resource IDs associated with the parent job run (commonly used to identify clusters for WORKFLOW_RUN).

columns.comment: The termination code of the job run. For possible values, see [Termination code values](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#termination). **Not populated for rows emitted before late August 2024.**
columns.business_meaning: System-provided code describing why/how the run ended (more specific than result_state).

columns.comment: The job-level parameters used in the job run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late August 2024.**
columns.business_meaning: Job-level parameter values supplied to the run, represented as a nested key/value structure.

columns.comment: The id of the source task run. Use for identifying which task run triggered this job run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
columns.business_meaning: Identifier of the task run that directly triggered this job run (for cross-run lineage).

columns.comment: The id of the root task run. Use for identifying which task run triggered this job run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
columns.business_meaning: Identifier of the originating/root task run responsible for triggering this job run (top of the triggering chain).

columns.comment: Details about the compute resources used in the job run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
columns.business_meaning: Structured details of compute used by the run, including compute type and referenced cluster_id/warehouse_id values.

columns.comment: The type of termination for the job run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
columns.business_meaning: High-level category of run termination (system-defined grouping, such as user/system/infra-related depending on values).

columns.comment: The duration of the setup phase for the job run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
columns.business_meaning: Seconds spent in run setup (for example, provisioning or initialization before execution).

columns.comment: The duration spent in the queue for the job run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
columns.business_meaning: Seconds the run spent waiting in queue before starting execution.

columns.comment: The total duration of the job run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
columns.business_meaning: Total end-to-end run duration in seconds.

columns.comment: The duration of the cleanup phase for the job run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
columns.business_meaning: Seconds spent in cleanup after execution completed.

columns.comment: The duration of the execution phase for the job run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
columns.business_meaning: Seconds spent executing the run work (excluding queue/setup/cleanup).

comment: The `job_task_run_timeline` table is a fact table that contains slices of ongoing job runs, allowing one to track the run time and metadata related to each job task run. Each slice is represented by `period_start_time` and `period_end_time` timestamps. For documentation, example queries, and monitoring usage, see [Lakeflow System Tables](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#job-task-run-timeline-table-schema).
business_context: Tracks job task run execution over time by emitting time-sliced records for each ongoing task run, enabling monitoring of duration, compute usage, and outcomes. It supports operational observability and cost/performance analysis for Databricks Jobs task runs.
grain: One row per job task run time slice, keyed by (account_id, workspace_id, job_id, run_id, task_key, period_start_time, period_end_time).
common_use_cases: Compute task run durations and identify long-running tasks by slicing and summing timeline periods
Analyze task outcomes (result_state/termination) over time and correlate failures with compute used
Attribute task run activity to clusters/warehouses and join to compute/node timelines for performance investigation
Build job/task run monitoring dashboards with time-windowed utilization and phase durations

columns.comment: The ID of the account this job belongs to.
The ID of the workspace this job belongs to.
The ID of the job. Only unique within a single workspace.
The ID of the task run.
columns.business_meaning: Identifier of the specific task run instance being time-sliced (task-level run).

columns.comment: The start time for the task or for the time period. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
columns.business_meaning: Start timestamp of the emitted timeline slice for the task run.

columns.comment: The end time for the task or for the time period. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
columns.business_meaning: End timestamp of the emitted timeline slice for the task run.

columns.comment: The reference key for a task in a job. This key is only unique within a single job.

columns.comment: An array containing the IDs of compute used by the job task.
columns.business_meaning: List of compute resource IDs used by the task run during the slice (may include multiple resources over time).

columns.comment: The outcome of the job task run.

comment: The `job_tasks` table is a slow-changing dimension table (SCD2) that contains the full history of job task configurations over time. When a row changes, a new row is emitted, logically replacing the previous one. For documentation, example queries, and monitoring usage, see [Lakeflow System Tables](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#job-task-table-schema).
business_context: Slow-changing dimension (SCD2) history of Databricks job task configuration for Lakeflow jobs, capturing every task definition change and deletion over time. Use it to audit how a job’s task graph and settings evolved within an account/workspace.

Reconstruct historical job task DAGs (dependencies) for a point-in-time view

comment: The `jobs` table is a slow-changing dimension table (SCD2) that contains the full history of job configurations over time. business_context: Slow-changing dimension (SCD2) history of Databricks Lakeflow job configurations, capturing every version of a job record when it is created, modified, paused, or deleted. comment: The `pipeline_update_timeline` table is a fact table that contains slices of ongoing pipeline updates, allowing one to track the execution time and metadata related to each pipeline update. Each slice is represented by `period_start_time` and `period_end_time` timestamps.

comment: The `pipelines` table is a slow-changing dimension table (SCD2) that contains the full history of pipeline configurations over time. When a row changes, a new row is emitted, logically replacing the previous one.
