columns.comment: The number of table partitions read after table partition pruning.
columns.business_meaning: Number of partitions read after partition pruning was applied.

columns.comment: The number of pruned files during table partition and file pruning.
columns.business_meaning: Count of files eliminated by partition and file pruning.

columns.comment: The number of files read after table partition and file pruning.
columns.business_meaning: Count of files actually read after partition/file pruning.

columns.comment: The number of rows output from scans after table partition, file, in file and row pruning.
columns.business_meaning: Number of rows produced by scan operators after pruning (table/file/row pruning effects applied).

columns.comment: Total number of rows returned by the statement.
columns.business_meaning: Total number of rows returned/produced by the statement result.

columns.comment: The number of bytes of files read after IO pruning.
columns.business_meaning: Bytes read from files after I/O pruning.

columns.comment: The percentage of bytes of persistent data read from the IO cache.
columns.business_meaning: Percent of persistent data bytes served from the I/O cache rather than remote storage.

columns.comment: TRUE indicates that the statement result was fetched from the cache.
columns.business_meaning: Indicates whether the statement result was served from the result cache.

columns.comment: Size of data, in bytes, temporarily written to disk while executing the statement.
columns.business_meaning: Bytes spilled to local disk during execution due to memory pressure.

columns.comment: The size in bytes of persistent data written to cloud object storage.
columns.business_meaning: Bytes of persistent data written to cloud object storage by the statement.

columns.comment: The total amount of data in bytes sent over the network.
columns.business_meaning: Bytes transferred over the network for shuffle reads (inter-node data exchange).

columns.comment: A struct that contains key-value pairs representing one or more Databricks entities that were involved in the execution of this statement, such as jobs, notebooks, or dashboards. This field only records Databricks entities and are not sorted by execution order. Statement executions that contain multiple IDs indicate that the execution was triggered by multiple entities: for example, an Alert may trigger on a Job result and call a SQL Query, so all three IDs will be populated within query_source.
columns.business_meaning: Structured references to Databricks entities involved in triggering/executing the statement (e.g., job runs, dashboards, alerts, notebooks, SQL queries, pipelines).

columns.comment: The ID of the user or service principal whose privilege was used to run the statement.
columns.business_meaning: Identity (user/service principal) whose privileges were used to execute the statement (impersonation/effective identity).

columns.comment: The name of the user or service principal whose privilege was used to run the statement.
columns.business_meaning: Name of the user or service principal whose privileges were used to execute the statement.

columns.comment: Total number of rows of persistent data written to cloud object storage.
columns.business_meaning: Number of persistent rows written to cloud object storage by the statement.

columns.comment: The number of files of persistent data written to cloud object storage.
columns.business_meaning: Number of persistent files written to cloud object storage by the statement.

columns.comment: Statement id of query that inserted result in cache when result is fetched from cache, otherwise store the query's statement id.
columns.business_meaning: Statement execution id that originally populated the cached result when this statement was served from cache; otherwise equals the current statement_id.

columns.comment: Query parameters values associated with the query. Only one of named_parameters or pos_parameters can be populated.
columns.business_meaning: Parameter bindings used for the statement execution (either named or positional), including typed literal representations and whether values were truncated.

columns.comment: The user-supplied custom tags associated with this statement execution.
columns.business_meaning: User-supplied key/value tags attached to the statement execution for categorization and attribution.

columns.comment: The number of bytes of files pruned after table partition and file pruning.
columns.business_meaning: Bytes of data eliminated by partition/file pruning (data not read).

columns.comment: The number of bytes of files read after table partition and file pruning.
columns.business_meaning: Bytes read from files after partition/file pruning.

comment: This system table records per request usage information for model serving. For instance, it can be used to attribute how many LLM tokens were sent to different served entities. To look up more information about the served entity that received the request, use the served_entity_id as the join key with system.serving.served_entities.
business_context: Per-request usage and attribution facts for Databricks Model Serving endpoints, including token/character counts and response status. Used to analyze and allocate serving consumption (especially LLM usage) by endpoint/served entity and caller context.
grain: One row per serving invocation request received by a served entity on a serving endpoint, identifiable by databricks_request_id (and optionally client_request_id) within a workspace.
common_use_cases: Attribute LLM token consumption (input/output) by served_entity_id, requester, and usage_context keys
Track request volumes and error rates over time using request_time and status_code
Estimate serving cost by joining to reference rate tables (for example cost rates) and aggregating usage measures
Operational analytics: compare streaming vs non-streaming request patterns and their associated usage

columns.comment: The workspace ID for the workspace in which the serving endpoint exists.
The Databricks account ID of the workspace associated with the serving endpoint.
The user provided request identifier that can be specified in the model serving request body.
columns.business_meaning: Caller-supplied request identifier provided in the model serving request payload for client-side tracing and deduplication.

columns.comment: A Databricks generated request identifier attached to all model serving requests.
columns.business_meaning: Databricks-generated unique request identifier for each model serving invocation, used for platform-side tracing and joining to other telemetry if available.

columns.comment: The ID of the user or service principal whose permissions are used for the invocation request of the serving endpoint.
columns.business_meaning: Identity (user or service principal) whose permissions were used to invoke the serving endpoint; represents the authenticated caller.

columns.comment: The HTTP status code that was returned from the model.
columns.business_meaning: HTTP response status code returned for the invocation, indicating success or error category.

columns.comment: The timestamp at which the request is received.
columns.business_meaning: Timestamp when Databricks received the serving request.

columns.comment: The token count of the input.
columns.business_meaning: Number of tokens in the request input (for example prompt tokens) attributed to this invocation.

columns.comment: The token count of the output.
columns.business_meaning: Number of tokens in the response output (for example completion tokens) attributed to this invocation.

columns.comment: The character count of the input string or prompt.
columns.business_meaning: Character length of the input string/prompt for this request.

columns.comment: The character count of the output string of the response.
columns.business_meaning: Character length of the response text produced for this request.

columns.comment: The user provided map containing identifiers of the end user or the customer application that makes the call to the endpoint. See [Further define usage with usage_context](https://docs.databricks.com/en/ai-gateway/configure-ai-gateway-endpoints.html#usage-context).
columns.business_meaning: User-provided key/value attributes describing the end user or calling application (for example tenant, app, customer identifiers) to support downstream attribution and chargeback.

columns.comment: Whether the request is in stream mode.
columns.business_meaning: Indicates whether the invocation used streaming responses (stream mode) versus a single non-streaming response.

columns.comment: The unique ID used to join with the system.serving.served_entities dimension table to lookup information about the endpoint and served entity.
columns.business_meaning: Identifier of the served entity that handled the request; join key to system.serving.served_entities to retrieve endpoint and served-entity metadata.

comment: This system table records all entities that were served through Databricks model serving as well as the configurations at different config versions.
business_context: Tracks every entity served via Databricks Model Serving and the served entity’s configuration as it changes across endpoint configuration versions. Useful for inventory, governance, and cost/usage attribution of serving endpoints and their deployed entities.
grain: One row per served entity per endpoint configuration version (and change event), uniquely identified by served_entity_id together with endpoint_config_version (and change_time for sequencing).
common_use_cases: Inventory all served entities by workspace/endpoint and entity type (custom, foundation, external, feature spec).
Audit configuration history over time (throughput/concurrency/compute) for a given endpoint or served entity.
Attribute serving usage and costs by joining to endpoint_usage and billing usage via served_entity_id/endpoint_id.
Identify when served entities were removed by endpoint deletion time for compliance and lifecycle management.

columns.comment: The unique ID of the served entity.
The Databricks account ID of the workspace associated with the serving endpoint.
The workspace ID for the workspace in which the serving endpoint exists.
The ID of the creator.
columns.business_meaning: Principal (user/service principal) identifier that created the served entity/endpoint configuration.

columns.comment: The name of the serving endpoint.
The unique ID of the serving endpoint.
The name of the served entity.
columns.business_meaning: User-assigned name/alias for the entity as deployed on the endpoint (may differ from the underlying UC model name).

columns.comment: Type of the entity that is served. Can be FEATURE_SPEC, EXTERNAL_MODEL, FOUNDATION_MODEL, or CUSTOM_MODEL
columns.business_meaning: Category of the served entity (FEATURE_SPEC, EXTERNAL_MODEL, FOUNDATION_MODEL, CUSTOM_MODEL).

columns.comment: The underlying name of the entity. Different from the served_entity_name which is a user provided name. For example, entity_name is the name of the Unity Catalog model.
columns.business_meaning: Canonical underlying entity name being served (for example, the Unity Catalog model name) rather than the user-provided served alias.

columns.comment: The version of the served entity.
columns.business_meaning: Version label/identifier of the underlying served entity (for example, a model version).

columns.comment: The version of the endpoint configuration.
columns.business_meaning: Monotonic version number of the endpoint configuration in which this served entity definition was active.

columns.comment: The task type. Can be llm/v1/chat, llm/v1/completions, or llm/v1/embeddings.
columns.business_meaning: Serving task/API mode used by the endpoint (llm/v1/chat, llm/v1/completions, llm/v1/embeddings).

columns.comment: Configurations for external models. For example, {Provider: OpenAI}
columns.business_meaning: Configuration payload specific to external model providers (for example, provider name such as OpenAI).

columns.comment: Configurations for foundation models. For example,{min_provisioned_throughput: 2200, max_provisioned_throughput: 4400}
columns.business_meaning: Configuration payload for foundation models, including provisioned throughput limits (min/max).

columns.comment: Configurations for custom models. For example,{ min_concurrency: 0, max_concurrency: 4, compute_type: CPU }
columns.business_meaning: Configuration payload for custom models, including concurrency limits and compute type.

columns.comment: Configurations for feature specifications. For example, { min_concurrency: 0, max_concurrency: 4, compute_type: CPU }
columns.business_meaning: Configuration payload for feature specification serving, including concurrency limits and compute type.

columns.comment: Timestamp of change for the served entity.
columns.business_meaning: Timestamp when this served entity record/config version became effective (change event time).

columns.comment: Timestamp of entity deletion. The endpoint is the container for the served entity. After the endpoint is deleted, the served entity is also deleted.
columns.business_meaning: Timestamp when the serving endpoint was deleted; after this time the contained served entities are considered deleted.

comment: Predictive optimization optimizes your data layout for peak performance and cost efficiency. This system table tracks the operation history of this feature.
business_context: Tracks the history of Predictive Optimization operations (e.g., compaction and vacuum) executed on tables, including timing, outcome, and associated usage for cost/chargeback analysis. Use it to audit optimization activity and understand operational impact across workspaces and metastores.
grain: One row per predictive optimization operation execution on a specific table (keyed by operation_id; associated to a workspace_id and table_id).
common_use_cases: Audit which tables were compacted or vacuumed, when, and whether the operations succeeded or failed
Analyze operational duration and cadence of predictive optimization by workspace/metastore/catalog/schema/table
Investigate failed optimization operations and correlate to specific tables or environments
Support usage reporting and chargeback using usage_unit and usage_quantity

columns.comment: ID of the account.
The ID of the workspace in which predictive optimization ran the operation.
The time at which the operation started. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
columns.business_meaning: Timestamp when the optimization operation began.

columns.comment: The time at which the operation ended. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
columns.business_meaning: Timestamp when the optimization operation completed (successfully or not).

columns.comment: The name of the metastore to which the optimized table belongs.
The ID of the metastore to which the optimized table belongs.
The name of the catalog to which the optimized table belongs.
The name of the schema to which the optimized table belongs.
The name of the optimized table.
The ID of the optimized table.
The optimization operation which was performed. The value will be COMPACTION or VACUUM.
columns.business_meaning: Type of predictive optimization action performed (e.g., COMPACTION or VACUUM).

columns.comment: The ID for the optimization operation.
columns.business_meaning: Unique identifier for a single predictive optimization operation execution.

columns.comment: The status of the optimization operation. The value will be SUCCESSFUL or FAILED: INTERNAL_ERROR.
columns.business_meaning: Outcome status of the operation (e.g., SUCCESSFUL or FAILED with an internal error classification).

columns.comment: Additional details about the specific optimization that was performed.
columns.business_meaning: Key-value metrics describing what the operation did and/or its effects (for example, files processed, bytes rewritten, or other operation-specific details).

columns.comment: The unit of usage that this operation incurred.
columns.business_meaning: Unit used to measure billable/attributed usage incurred by the optimization operation.

columns.comment: The amount of the usage unit that was used by this operation.
columns.business_meaning: Amount of usage incurred by the operation in the specified usage_unit.
